selector.exact: The great events of history were not really achieved by peoples; they themselves created the peoples.

annotations.anchor: the 'people' is a unit of the soul … the great events … created the peoples
annotations.note: Spengler's definition, completing the demolition of chapter V: 'the people is a unit of the soul' — not a race, a language, or a political body, but a community held together by an inner form. And the order of cause runs backward from the usual story: 'the great events of history were not really achieved by peoples; they themselves created the peoples.' It was the migrations that made the Ostrogoths a people; the events came first, the people after. A people is a result, not a precondition.

selector.exact: A Jew who went over to the Mazda faith became thereby a Persian

annotations.anchor: A Jew who went over to the Mazda faith became thereby a Persian
annotations.note: A pointed example that 'people' is soul, not blood. In Parthian–Sassanid times 'not tribal attachment but orthodoxy' became 'the hall-mark of nationality': 'A Jew who went over to the Mazda faith became thereby a Persian; a Persian who became a Christian belonged to the Nestorian "people."' The Persians, Spengler notes, were once 'Aryans, but no people,' then 'a people, but without race.' Nationality here is a matter of belief and inner form — the very opposite of the descent-based 'race' his contemporaries meant.

selector.exact: Peoples in the style of their Culture we will call Nations

annotations.anchor: Peoples in the style of their Culture we will call Nations … underlying the nation there is an Idea
annotations.note: Spengler's term for the highest people-form: a 'nation' is a people living 'in the style of' a high Culture, distinguished from the primitive peoples before it and the fellaheen after. What forges it is not merely a feeling of 'we' but 'an Idea' — 'a very deep relation to Destiny, to Time, and to History.' This is why, for him, the modern nation is not eternal but datable: it appears with its Culture and fades with it.

selector.exact: Out of the people-shapes of the Carolingian Empire

annotations.anchor: Out of the people-shapes of the Carolingian Empire … arise suddenly the German, the French, the Spaniards, the Italians
annotations.note: The decisive reversal. Historians treat the Culture-peoples — German, French, Spanish, Italian — as 'primaries' and the Culture as their product. Spengler turns it around: 'in the tenth century the Faustian soul suddenly awoke,' and out of the older Carolingian tribal shapes (Saxons, Swabians, Franks, Lombards) it created the modern nations. The Culture is the cause; the nation is the form its awakening takes. Peoples are how a Culture appears in the world, not its raw material.

The chapter's three ages of a people are primitives (before the Culture), nations (the living Culture), and fellaheen (after it). The 'fellaheen' are post-historical peoples — the late Egyptians among the pyramids, the late Chinese, post-Actium Hellenism — whose Culture is spent and who live on without history, swayed by 'fellah-instincts.' The name is borrowed from the Egyptian fellaheen, the peasants who endured among the ruins of the pharaohs. For Spengler the West, too, is bound for this condition; it is the human end-state of his whole morphology.

selector.exact: nations of Faustian style emerge, more and more distinctly, from the time of Otto the Great

annotations.anchor: nations of Faustian style emerge … from the time of Otto the Great
annotations.note: Each Culture, true to Spengler's method, makes nations in its own image, and the three are incommensurable. The Classical nation is a body of citizens bound to a Polis (Apollinian — the present, the body); the Magian/Arabian nation is a community of believers in which 'orthodoxy' defines nationality (the convert changes people); the Faustian nation is a dynamic, idea-bearing people, emerging from about Otto the Great (936–973) and feeling itself German, French, Spanish by A.D. 1000. Three different things wear the one word 'nation.'
